name: Fukuoka / Itoshima
panel_summary: The panel would treat this as the most practical “use it, rent it, live in it” candidate: not the most dramatic scenery, but the combination of airport access, food, safety, healthcare and clean ownership is unusually strong.
pros: resilient domestic and regional travel demand
relatively affordable versus global resort markets.
cons: Less scarce and less trophy-like than prime ski, lake or island markets
STR upside is capped by regulation and asset type
beachfront scarcity varies sharply by micro-location
panel_verdict: Keep as a top-tier shortlist candidate. It is the “highest probability of working” option rather than the most romantic one.
ownership_notes: Open Japan freehold ownership; standard Japanese transaction and STR compliance.
red_flags: Less scarce/trophy than resort markets; STR upside lower than ski/beach villas.

rental.gross_yield: Japan average gross yield 4.55%; Fukuoka STR revenue supports mid-single-digit gross underwriting for good units
rental.revenue: AirROI 2026: ~$24,963 average annual Airbnb revenue
rental.confidence: Medium-high for STR metrics; medium for net yield

price_basis: Built residential benchmark. Early-2026 Fukuoka average housing price ~¥422,000/m²; converted at 1 USD = ¥161.305 => ~$2,616/m². Itoshima varies materially by distance to beach/station.
price_confidence: Medium-high; city benchmark, not resort-villa specific
profit_driver: Excellent airport access, food/lifestyle, Korean/Chinese/Taiwanese travel, domestic migration and urban economy.

name: Valencia
panel_summary: This is one of the cleanest European retirement/liveability candidates. It wins on food, healthcare, cost, culture and year-round demand, but it is more lifestyle city than trophy holiday resort.
cons: STR rules and housing politics require local diligence
less visually spectacular than lake/alpine/island markets
Chinese buyer ecosystem is smaller than in major global gateway cities.
panel_verdict: Keep near the top. Best suited for retirement optionality and long-stay demand, not ultra-luxury holiday yield.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy Spanish property. Tourist lets and licences are municipality/region-specific; city-centre STR rules require careful checking.
red_flags: Housing-policy pressure, licence restrictions, rising prices, less trophy-scenery than island/lake markets.

rental.gross_yield: Spain city yields are generally mid-single digit gross; Valencia benefits from affordability versus Madrid/Barcelona
rental.revenue: May 2026 Valencia asking prices around €3,342/m²; local reporting flags transaction prices above €2,700/m² and listings above €3,300/m².
rental.adr_occupancy: City/coastal ADR moderate / Strong year-round, less pure-holiday seasonal

price_basis: Built residential benchmark. Valencia May 2026 asking price ~€3,342/m² × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $3,836/m².
profit_driver: Best European retirement/liveability/cost balance: food, healthcare, airport, beach, culture and year-round city demand.

name: Algarve / Cascais
panel_summary: A proven retirement and second-home market with clean ownership and strong lifestyle appeal. The panel would like the risk-adjusted case, but would separate Cascais from Algarve in deeper diligence because economics and liquidity differ.
cons: Short-term rental licensing can be restrictive
yields are usually moderate after management and taxes
prime coastal areas are no longer cheap.
panel_verdict: Keep as a core European benchmark. Strong for retirement and lifestyle, only average for development yield.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy freehold property. STRs require Alojamento Local registration/licensing and municipalities retain control in pressure zones.
red_flags: AL licence/local restrictions, rising prices, water/heat risk, lower Asia proximity.

rental.gross_yield: Portugal average gross rental yield ~4.29% Q2 2026; Lisbon/Porto STR sources cite 5–10% gross in strong cities
rental.revenue: Location-specific STR data needed; Cascais/Algarve depend heavily on licence and asset quality
rental.adr_occupancy: N/A / N/A

price_basis: Blended built-property benchmark. Algarve 2026 regional references range roughly €3,139–4,050/m²; Cascais around €4,654/m². Converted at 1 EUR = 1.14784 USD; blended dashboard figure rounded to ~$4,600/m².
price_confidence: Medium-high; region blend hides prime coastal dispersion
profit_driver: Retirement migration, European access, golf/beach, tax/visa legacy demand, strong liveability.

name: Málaga / Costa del Sol
panel_summary: This is a high-conviction lifestyle/retirement market because it has airport scale, healthcare, beach, food and a large expat ecosystem. The issue is whether you are buying after too much price appreciation.
cons: STR and anti-tourism politics need careful monitoring
asset quality and micro-location dispersion are large.
panel_verdict: Keep, but require strict entry-price discipline. Good destination; not necessarily good at any price.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy Spanish property, but Andalusia/municipal STR registration and community rules should be verified.
red_flags: Rapid price inflation, anti-tourism politics, summer heat/water, lower scarcity outside prime zones.

rental.gross_yield: Good tourist demand and expat rental base; yields vary sharply by licence and micro-location
rental.revenue: 2026 local reporting shows Málaga luxury coastal districts above €8,000/m²; broader city/province is lower but rising quickly.
rental.adr_occupancy: Strong for licensed coastal units/villas / Strong year-round, especially city + coast

price_basis: Blended built-property proxy. Prime Málaga/Costa del Sol districts exceed €8,000/m²; broader quality product is lower. Dashboard uses a mid-prime blended proxy (~€4,880/m² × EUR/USD).
profit_driver: Large airport, healthcare, golf/beach, retirement ecosystem, growing digital/expat demand.

name: Lake Como
panel_summary: A beautiful, globally recognised lake market with Milan access. The panel would view it as a lifestyle and capital-preservation candidate rather than a yield-led development market.
pros: good exit liquidity for prime assets.
cons: summer seasonality can be sharp outside the main towns
panel_verdict: Keep for prestige and long-term liquidity. Do not rank it as a yield destination unless a very specific asset is mispriced.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy Italian property. STR/tourist rental rules, condominium consent and local tax treatment need asset-level diligence.
red_flags: Lower yield, prime lakefront scarcity/price, older buildings, limited winter demand in some villages.

rental.gross_yield: Residential gross yield usually low-to-mid single digit; lakefront trophy assets are appreciation/liquidity stories more than yield stories
rental.revenue: 2026 Lake Como references show median about €3,400/m² and average about €4,050/m², with lakefront/prime materially higher.
rental.adr_occupancy: Strong in summer; luxury villa ADR can be high / Seasonal; better in larger towns

price_basis: Built residential benchmark. 2026 Lake Como sources cite median ~€3,400/m² and average ~€4,050/m²; dashboard uses average × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $4,649/m².
price_confidence: Medium; prime lakefront can be far higher
profit_driver: Milan access, global luxury brand, lake/mountain scenery, restaurants, boating and long-stay quality.

name: Hakone / Izu
panel_summary: This is the Tokyo-adjacent lifestyle option: easier to use often, cleaner to own, and more resilient domestically than many pure tourism plays. The panel would like it for optionality but question international demand depth.
pros: lower entry price than prime global resorts
cons: less global brand pull than Niseko/Hakuba
panel_verdict: Keep as a practical Japan alternative. Best for personal use and domestic demand, less compelling as a global trophy asset.
ownership_notes: Clean Japanese freehold ownership; municipality-specific STR compliance is the key diligence item.
red_flags: Older stock, earthquake/typhoon risk, less international trophy appeal.

rental.gross_yield: Japan broad gross yield reference: ~4.55%; resort STR varies widely
rental.revenue: No clean 2026 local STR benchmark found; underwrite with Japan city/resort comps
rental.adr_occupancy: N/A / N/A

price_basis: Built-property/listing proxy. Hakone active examples range from roughly ¥425,000/m² to ¥700,000/m²+ of building area; Izu can be much cheaper. Converted at 1 USD = ¥161.305; dashboard uses a broad Tokyo-adjacent resort proxy.
price_confidence: Low-medium; local stock is heterogeneous and old homes distort averages
profit_driver: Tokyo weekend demand, onsen/nature stays, easier personal use, domestic tourism resilience.

name: Madeira
panel_summary: Madeira deserves a place because it has year-round climate, scenery and improving remote-work/retirement demand. The panel would like the lifestyle story but mark down island liquidity and healthcare depth versus mainland cities.
pros: still relatively affordable versus prime Europe
appealing to retirees and digital nomads.
cons: medical complexity for serious conditions may require mainland Portugal
STR/licensing and local politics need review.
panel_verdict: Keep as a differentiated Europe water/nature candidate. Attractive, but size positions conservatively because liquidity is thinner.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy Portuguese property. STRs require Alojamento Local registration/licensing and municipality checks.
red_flags: Island liquidity/airlift risk, AL/local restrictions, limited medical depth vs mainland, landslide/flood terrain risk.

rental.gross_yield: Potentially attractive gross yield in Funchal/tourist zones if AL licence is secured
rental.revenue: Portugal price references put prime/Lisbon much higher; Madeira/Funchal has risen sharply and commonly screens around mid-€3k/m² for quality stock.
rental.adr_occupancy: Moderate to strong / Good year-round climate; island airlift dependent

price_basis: Built-property proxy. Dashboard uses ~€3,485/m² × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $4,000/m² for quality Madeira/Funchal stock; verify local neighbourhood pricing.
profit_driver: Year-round mild climate, hiking, ocean views, retirement/digital-nomad demand, lower entry than prime Med islands.

name: Costa Brava / Girona
panel_summary: This is a strong understated coastal candidate: better food and access than many beach markets, less obvious than Mallorca, and close to Barcelona/Girona. The panel would like it if the exact town avoids over-tourism and regulatory friction.
cons: lower Chinese familiarity than Spain’s major cities.
panel_verdict: Keep. One of the better “quality of life plus accessible coast” European options.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy Spanish property. Catalonia/local tourist licence restrictions are a core diligence item.
red_flags: Licence scarcity, summer seasonality, Catalonia regulation, lower liquidity outside famous villages.

rental.gross_yield: Licensed coastal villas can rent well, but rules and seasonality cap underwriting
rental.revenue: 2026 sources cite Begur asking around €4,000/m² and Girona houses/apartments around €2,283–2,653/m².
rental.adr_occupancy: High in summer for licensed prime coastal villas / Seasonal, better with Girona/Barcelona-adjacent use

price_basis: Prime coastal proxy. Begur ~€4,000/m² × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $4,591/m²; Girona city averages are materially lower.
profit_driver: Food, scenery, Barcelona/Girona access, less obvious than Mallorca, strong European second-home demand.

name: Crete
panel_summary: Crete is attractive because it combines affordability, food, beaches, history and a real local population. The panel would like the value angle but question liquidity and seasonality outside the best towns.
cons: infrastructure and healthcare vary by area
resale liquidity is thinner outside prime towns.
panel_verdict: Keep as a value-oriented Europe candidate. Strong for lifestyle, but underwrite conservatively for exit and operations.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy Greek property, subject to normal legal checks and some border-area/security restrictions. STR registration/tax compliance required.
red_flags: Seasonality, fragmented healthcare outside cities, wildfire/heat/water risk, lower liquidity in rural stock.

rental.gross_yield: Gross yields can be decent in Chania/Heraklion/coastal tourist zones; net depends on season and operator
rental.revenue: Crete May 2026 asking prices around €2,578/m²; coastal Chania/Heraklion examples can run €2,000–4,000/m².
rental.adr_occupancy: Seasonal but long shoulder season / Good long season, weaker winter in resort-only zones

price_basis: Built residential benchmark. Crete May 2026 average asking price ~€2,578/m² × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $2,959/m².
profit_driver: Food, scenery, lower entry cost, long warm season, improving retirement case.

name: Hakuba
panel_summary: Hakuba is the more affordable and potentially earlier-stage Japan ski thesis versus Niseko. The panel would like the upside but demand a serious execution and seasonality discount.
panel_verdict: Keep as an upside candidate. It is more venture-like than Fukuoka or Algarve: higher upside, higher operating risk.
ownership_notes: Japan freehold ownership is generally open to foreigners; STR route needs local licence/notification and operational compliance.
red_flags: Less mature operating ecosystem than Niseko, lower liquidity, execution quality uneven.

rental.gross_yield: 5–8% gross STR potential for well-located chalets/condos
rental.revenue: AirROI 2026: ~$47,484 average annual Airbnb revenue
rental.confidence: Medium-high for STR metrics; medium for yield

price_basis: Built chalet/listing proxy. Active 2026 examples imply roughly ¥0.7m–1.6m/m² of building area depending on chalet quality and land bundle; converted at 1 USD = ¥161.305. Land-only Iwatake reference around ¥49,000/m² is shown as a development input, not the comparable buyable-property metric.
price_confidence: Medium-low; active-listing proxy, property mix varies widely
profit_driver: Lower entry than Niseko, strong winter upside, growing summer hiking/biking demand.

name: Annecy
panel_summary: Annecy is one of the best lake-plus-mountain lifestyle markets in Europe. The panel would rank it highly for liveability, but not for affordability or yield.
pros: cycling, hiking, skiing and lake activities
cons: STR rules and local housing pressure require diligence
smaller Chinese ecosystem than global gateways.
panel_verdict: Keep as a premium lifestyle contender. Excellent to own; hard to make the numbers exciting.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy French property, but France has tightened furnished tourist-let registration/authorisation and building-level restrictions.
red_flags: STR regulation, high entry price, local housing pressure, France tax/admin complexity.

rental.gross_yield: High prices compress yield; STR attractive only if compliant and well located
rental.revenue: Annecy residential price references show average about €7,599/m² in 2026; luxury average around €8,093/m².
rental.adr_occupancy: Good ADR in lake/summer and alpine shoulder season / Year-round, but summer and ski access drive peaks

price_basis: Built-property benchmark. Annecy average ~€7,599/m² × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $8,722/m².
profit_driver: Exceptional lake/mountain lifestyle, Geneva/Lyon access, healthcare and retirement quality, summer + ski-adjacent demand.

name: Mallorca
panel_summary: Mallorca is proven, liquid and beautiful, with strong food and airport access. The panel would recognise the quality but worry about regulation, crowding and entry price.
pros: beaches, cycling, hiking, food and culture
cons: prime areas are expensive and crowded.
panel_verdict: Keep, but only if licence and micro-location are exceptional. It is a mature market, not a bargain market.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy Spanish property, but Balearic holiday-rental rules are among Spain’s strictest and are zone/property-specific.
red_flags: Tourist rental licence scarcity, anti-tourism politics, summer crowding, heat/water risk.

rental.gross_yield: Palma de Mallorca gross residential yield ~4.41%; Mallorca island yield references around ~4.8–4.9%
rental.revenue: STR revenue can be high for licensed coastal villas; new tourist licences are the gating factor
rental.adr_occupancy: N/A / N/A
rental.confidence: Medium for residential yield; low-medium for STR yield

price_basis: Built-property benchmark. 2026 Mallorca references show houses around €4,704/m² and apartments around €5,384/m²; Balearic second-hand data around €5,300/m². Converted at 1 EUR = 1.14784 USD; rounded dashboard figure ~$6,100/m².
price_confidence: Medium-high; island average, prime coastal/licensed villas can be much higher
profit_driver: European wealth, lifestyle/retirement demand, sailing, beaches, food, long season.

name: Croatia / Istria-Dalmatia
panel_summary: Croatia offers beautiful coastlines and improving EU-market credibility at lower prices than Western Europe. The panel would like the value but mark down legal/admin complexity and seasonality.
pros: still cheaper than Italy/France/Spain prime coast
cons: liquidity is thinner outside prime towns
panel_verdict: Watchlist. Attractive value, but needs sharper local partner and legal diligence than Spain/Portugal.
ownership_notes: EU property market with generally clearer ownership than many EM destinations; local legal/title checks still important.
red_flags: Seasonality, smaller buyer pool than Spain/Italy, title/permit diligence, liquidity outside prime towns.

rental.gross_yield: Tourist demand strong in prime coast; yields depend heavily on season, licence and management
rental.revenue: May 2026 Croatia data shows Dalmatia around €4,089/m²; Istria references around €2,500–4,000/m² depending view/location.
rental.adr_occupancy: High in summer / Seasonal, better in larger towns

price_basis: Built-property coastal proxy. Dalmatia ~€4,089/m² × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $4,693/m²; Istria can be lower or similar in prime towns.
profit_driver: Adriatic scenery, EU access, still cheaper than many Western Med peers, improving infrastructure.

name: Niseko
panel_summary: Niseko remains the best-known Asia-Pacific ski trophy market, but the corrected built-price data changes the case. The panel would admire the brand but challenge the yield and entry valuation.
cons: heavy winter dependence despite summer potential
exit liquidity is narrower than major cities.
panel_verdict: Keep as a trophy/specialist candidate, not a default top pick. Needs asset-specific edge to justify the price.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally own land and buildings freehold in Japan. STR requires either minpaku notification, hotel/ryokan route, or local-compliant structure. Minpaku is capped nationally at 180 days/year and local rules can be tighter.
red_flags: High build costs, operator dependency, snow/climate sensitivity, local pushback if overtourism rises.

rental.gross_yield: 6–10% STR gross; some 2026 analyses cite ~10% gross for selected resort properties
rental.revenue: Niseko-specific paid/commercial datasets vary; peak winter occupancy often high, but owner usage and OPEX materially reduce net returns
rental.adr_occupancy: High; property/operator-specific / High in winter; shoulder season improving

price_basis: Built-property benchmark, not land. Hirafu condominium average sales price reported at ~$14,644/m². For development reference only: official/land benchmarks are far lower and highly location-sensitive, from Niseko town land to prime Hirafu plots.
price_confidence: Medium; built-condo benchmark in Hirafu, land/villa economics vary materially
profit_driver: Premium winter ADR, global Asian/Australian ski demand, branded residences, summer golf/cycling/wellness extension.

name: Queenstown
panel_summary: Queenstown is a world-class scenery and activity market, but the foreign-buyer restriction is a major structural problem. The panel would only proceed if the ownership pathway is clearly available.
cons: remote from Asia/global business centres versus European city-adjacent markets.
panel_verdict: Specialist only. Beautiful and high quality, but ownership constraints make it hard to rank near the top.
ownership_notes: Overseas people usually cannot buy residential land unless eligible/consented; Australian and Singaporean citizens have specific pathways. Development or visa-linked exceptions require legal diligence.
red_flags: Foreign-buyer restrictions, expensive entry, housing politics, limited local labour.

rental.gross_yield: STR gross varies by property; high ADR supports mid-single-digit gross in strong assets
rental.revenue: AirROI 2026: ~$63,334 average annual Airbnb revenue
rental.confidence: High for STR metrics; medium for acquisition yield

price_basis: Improved-property/build-cost proxy rather than clean market €/m² equivalent. Queenstown average property value was NZ$1.94m in May 2026, while high-end build-cost proxy is NZ$6,000–8,000/m²; converted at 1 NZD = 0.5737 USD. Land/location premium can push effective cost higher.
price_confidence: Low-medium; no clean public sale-price/m² series found
profit_driver: Global adventure tourism, scarce lake/mountain land, strong ADR, four-season demand.

name: Phuket / Koh Samui
net_yield_estimate: 3–6% est. net; leasehold risk should require higher hurdle rate

quick_metrics.net_yield: 3–6% est. net; leasehold risk should require higher hurdle rate

panel_summary: Thailand is compelling on yield and lifestyle but weak on land ownership clarity. The panel would view it as an operating/yield play, not a clean retirement-property ownership play.
pros: food, services and healthcare are good in key areas
cons: Foreigners generally cannot own land freehold
governance and exit liquidity vary widely.
panel_verdict: Keep as a yield candidate only with conservative legal structuring. Not a clean core holding.
ownership_notes: Foreigners cannot own Thai land freehold. Condos can be freehold within the 49% foreign quota; villas typically use leasehold/superficies/company structures with legal risk.
red_flags: Land-title/leasehold economics, illegal nominee/company structures, oversupply pockets, monsoon/maintenance.

rental.gross_yield: 4–8% gross often cited; trophy villas can exceed but results are operator/location-specific
rental.net_yield: 3–6% est. net; leasehold risk should require higher hurdle rate
rental.revenue: AirROI Phuket: ~$12,292 avg annual revenue, $162 ADR, 37.7% occupancy. AirDNA/Airbtics Koh Samui snapshots: ~56–66% occupancy; Samui median revenue around THB1.26m per listing cited by local analysis.
rental.adr_occupancy: Phuket $162; Samui sources $140–254 / Phuket 37.7%; Samui ~56–66%
rental.confidence: High for STR metrics; medium for actual villa yield

price_basis: USD-denominated project benchmark. Phuket 2026 data shows condos around $2,000–3,400/m² for freehold units in many areas and higher new-project apartment averages around $4,100–4,220/m²; villas can be around ~$2,390/m². Dashboard uses blended villa/condo entry proxy.
price_confidence: Medium; zone/tenure mix matters a lot
profit_driver: Asian tourism, wellness, high villa ADR, strong operating ecosystem, relatively low construction/labour costs.

name: Vancouver Island / Victoria
panel_summary: A calmer North American water-and-retirement benchmark with healthcare, English-language ease, and lifestyle depth, but Canada foreign-buyer/tax rules and BC housing policy require care.
pros: more practical than trophy US resorts
cons: Canadian foreign-buyer restrictions and taxes can apply
lower short-term-rental upside in many areas
panel_verdict: Add as the North American retirement-water benchmark. Strong lifestyle fit, but policy and tax diligence matter.
ownership_notes: Canada generally has clear title, but foreign-buyer restrictions, vacancy/speculation taxes, and provincial/municipal rules must be checked before assuming eligibility.
red_flags: Foreign-buyer policy, BC speculation/vacancy taxes, STR restrictions, and lower yield after ownership costs.

rental.gross_yield: Long-term rental demand is supported by livability and constrained supply; STR upside is more policy-sensitive.
rental.revenue: Use long-stay/retirement housing demand as base case, not aggressive STR.
rental.adr_occupancy: Moderate year-round demand; summer tourism peak.

price_basis: Victoria/Vancouver Island built residential benchmark. Approx. US$630/sq ft equivalent, converted to about $6,800/m2; waterfront and Victoria core can be higher.
profit_driver: Retirement suitability, healthcare/livability, English-language ease, and water-oriented lifestyle scarcity.

name: Dolomites / South Tyrol
panel_summary: This is arguably Europe’s strongest mountain lifestyle candidate on scenery, food and year-round activity. The panel would like the quality but challenge affordability, yields and local restrictions.
cons: permitting and second-home restrictions can be tight
panel_verdict: Keep as a premium mountain benchmark. Great lifestyle asset, but financial return depends on buying unusually well.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy Italian property, but local planning, second-home sentiment, condominium rules and tourist-rental compliance matter.
red_flags: Expensive, lower yield, local planning friction, fragmented operating market, high build/specification costs.

rental.gross_yield: Premium alpine gross yields usually compressed by high prices; underwrite as lifestyle-first unless tourist-let permit is strong
rental.revenue: Cortina/Dolomites prime market; UBS 2026 European Alpine Property Focus cites Cortina premium segment just under CHF12,000 / EUR12,500 per m².
rental.adr_occupancy: High in peak ski/summer; property-specific / Strong winter and summer, but seasonal

price_basis: Premium built-property benchmark. UBS 2026 cites Cortina/Dolomites premium segment just under CHF12,000 / EUR12,500 per m²; dashboard uses EUR12,500 × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $14,348/m².
price_confidence: Medium; premium benchmark not all South Tyrol
profit_driver: UNESCO scenery, skiing, hiking/cycling, best-in-class food, proximity to Innsbruck/Venice/Verona/Milan corridors.

name: Bali
net_yield_estimate: 3.5–6.5% est. net; only attractive with strong operator and conservative lease math

quick_metrics.net_yield: 3.5–6.5% est. net; only attractive with strong operator and conservative lease math

panel_summary: Bali is high-yield and high-demand, but the ownership structure is the Achilles’ heel. The panel would treat it as a hospitality operating thesis rather than a straightforward property investment.
cons: Foreigners cannot own freehold land directly
exit depends heavily on structure and location.
panel_verdict: Specialist/yield bucket. Worth studying, but only with excellent legal/operator control and higher required return.
ownership_notes: Foreigners generally cannot own land freehold. Structures include leasehold, Hak Pakai/HGB through eligible entities, or PT PMA; legal/tax setup is central to the return.
red_flags: Leasehold terminal value, oversupply, traffic/infrastructure, regulatory/tax enforcement, operator quality.

rental.gross_yield: High single-digit gross often marketed; 2026 data shows saturation pressure
rental.net_yield: 3.5–6.5% est. net; only attractive with strong operator and conservative lease math
rental.revenue: Airbtics Bali 2026: ADR about IDR1.46m (~$90) and 63% median occupancy; market commentary flags listing growth and yield compression.
rental.adr_occupancy: ~$90 median / ~63% median

price_basis: Built-villa benchmark. 2026 Bali sources show villa pricing roughly $1,060–2,480/m² depending on area and quality, with compact apartments sometimes $2,600–3,520/m². Dashboard uses a villa-development-oriented proxy.
price_confidence: Medium; leasehold/freehold/entity structure affects true economic value
profit_driver: Global nomad/wellness demand, high utilisation in prime zones, brandable villas, strong hospitality ecosystem.

name: Chamonix
panel_summary: Chamonix is a true global alpine trophy destination with year-round outdoor demand. The panel would respect the brand but mark it down hard on price and yield.
pros: skiing, climbing, hiking and trail running
cons: local STR rules and renovation constraints
not cheap enough for the risk-adjusted score to lead.
panel_verdict: Keep as a benchmark, not a priority acquisition unless the asset is exceptional.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can generally buy French property, but Chamonix/France have tightened tourist-let rules. From 2025/26, registration/authorisation and building-level STR bans can matter.
red_flags: Short-term rental authorisation risk, tax changes, local anti-second-home pressure.

rental.gross_yield: STR revenue strong but price and regulation compress yield
rental.revenue: AirROI 2026: ~$36,531–36,980 average annual Airbnb revenue; Airbtics cites ~€44k median
rental.adr_occupancy: $352–360 / 42.1–44.8% by AirROI; Airbtics cites 65% median

price_basis: Built-property benchmark. Chamonix-Mont-Blanc average property price about €13,833/m²; converted at 1 EUR = 1.14784 USD => ~$15,877/m².
profit_driver: Global alpine brand, Geneva access, mountaineering, skiing, summer outdoor sports.

name: Park City / Deer Valley
panel_summary: A practical US mountain-market benchmark with better airport access and broader product depth than many trophy resorts, but still expensive and regulation-sensitive.
pros: Strong Salt Lake City airport access
good liquidity versus smaller ski towns
cons: snow/climate variability over long holding periods
US tax and estate planning complexity for foreign buyers
panel_verdict: Worth adding as the most practical US ski benchmark. More investable than Aspen, but not cheap.
ownership_notes: US freehold ownership is open to foreign buyers; STR permissions depend heavily on zoning, HOA, and resort area.
red_flags: High prices, STR zoning/HOA friction, traffic/crowding, and winter-season concentration.

rental.gross_yield: Strong ski-season gross demand in permitted zones; underwriting must separate gross revenue from HOA, management, tax, and maintenance.
rental.revenue: Asset and zone-specific; verify with local STR manager before underwriting.
rental.adr_occupancy: High winter peak, summer shoulder support.

price_basis: Built residential benchmark using Park City/Deer Valley luxury and resort product. Approx. $1,300/sq ft blended benchmark, converted to about $14,000/m2.
profit_driver: Airport access, major resort infrastructure, Deer Valley expansion, and broader liquidity than smaller mountain towns.

name: Da Nang / Hoi An
panel_summary: Da Nang/Hoi An scores well on affordability and regional tourism, but the ownership structure and exit market are weaker. The panel would view it as value-plus-growth, not institutional-quality core.
pros: good food and lower cost of living.
cons: STR rules and building management can change.
panel_verdict: Watchlist. Cheap is not enough; only proceed with a very clean title/project and strong operator.
ownership_notes: Foreigners can buy in approved commercial residential projects but generally cannot own land; common term is 50-year leasehold with quotas such as 30% of condo units.
red_flags: Leasehold/quota limits, weaker exit liquidity, developer/title risk, apartment STR restrictions can change.

rental.gross_yield: STR demand visible, but foreign-title constraints mean higher required return
rental.revenue: AirROI Da Nang: ~$8,278 annual revenue, $88 ADR, 39.9% occupancy. Airbtics Da Nang/Hoi An cites higher median occupancy around 51–64% depending market.
rental.adr_occupancy: $88 Da Nang; Hoi An lower/mid market varies / 39.9% AirROI Da Nang; 51–64% other snapshots
rental.confidence: Medium for STR metrics; low-medium for foreign-investor yield

price_basis: USD-denominated apartment benchmark. Da Nang apartment references around $1,400–2,000/m²; prime beachfront new launches can reach $2,500–3,550/m². Dashboard uses mid-market foreign-accessible apartment proxy.
profit_driver: Beachfront tourism, improving infrastructure, affordability, Korean/Chinese/regional arrivals.

name: Whistler
panel_summary: Whistler is an elite resort with real four-season depth, but Canada’s foreign-buyer rules and high price make it difficult for this mandate. The panel would not put it in the top tier for a foreign buyer.
pros: World-class ski and summer activity market
cons: far from Asian business hubs compared with Japan.
panel_verdict: Watchlist/specialist. Excellent destination, poor fit unless legal eligibility and price are unusually favourable.
ownership_notes: Canada’s federal foreign-buyer ban is extended to 1 Jan 2027, but rules apply based on census areas and property types; BC resort exceptions and local zoning need legal confirmation.
red_flags: High price compression, vacancy/foreign taxes, zoning limits, political sensitivity.

rental.gross_yield: Typically compressed by high prices; underwriting often low-to-mid single digit gross
rental.revenue: AirDNA public snapshot: Whistler STR market metrics vary by zone/property; high ADR but expensive entry
rental.adr_occupancy: High / Seasonal, strong winter/summer

price_basis: Built-property proxy. Whistler price-per-square-foot references of roughly CAD$1,100–1,600/sq ft imply ~$8,350–12,170/m² at 1 CAD = 0.70609 USD; dashboard uses midpoint rounded to ~$10,260/m².
profit_driver: Deep global ski brand, Vancouver access, summer biking, institutional-quality resort ecosystem.

name: Andermatt
panel_summary: Andermatt is the most investable Swiss alpine option for foreigners because of its exemption structure. The panel would still mark it down for valuation and low yields.
panel_verdict: Best Swiss option for a foreign buyer, but more capital-preservation/lifestyle than profit-maximisation.
ownership_notes: Andermatt Swiss Alps has special exemption from Lex Koller restrictions for eligible development purchases, making it unusually accessible to non-resident foreign buyers. Verify exact project/unit status.
red_flags: Still expensive, development concentration risk, resort execution dependence, low net yield.

rental.gross_yield: Better rental optionality than many Swiss resorts but still price-compressed
rental.revenue: UBS 2026 cites Andermatt around CHF22,000/m². Andermatt is widely cited as a Lex Koller-exempt exception for foreign buyers.
rental.adr_occupancy: High in ski season, improving summer / Improving four-season resort demand

price_basis: Premium resort benchmark. UBS 2026 cites Andermatt around CHF22,000/m² × CHF/USD 1.24288 ≈ $27,343/m².
profit_driver: Cleaner foreign-buyer access than most Switzerland, strong infrastructure investment, snow/mountain brand.

name: Innsbruck / Tyrol
panel_summary: Innsbruck/Tyrol is excellent for liveability and alpine access, but Austrian second-home and tourist-let restrictions are a serious barrier. The panel would admire it but keep it below cleaner jurisdictions.
cons: rental permission is asset-specific and often restrictive.
panel_verdict: Keep as a lifestyle benchmark, not a leading development candidate unless a legally rentable asset is identified.
ownership_notes: Austria is restrictive for second homes and holiday rentals, especially in Tyrol. Confirm main-residence, leisure-residence and tourist-rental status before underwriting.
red_flags: Foreign/second-home restrictions, low yields, high prices, limited true tourist-let stock.

rental.gross_yield: Yield compressed; tourist-let permits can create a large value split between rentable and non-rentable stock
rental.revenue: 2026 Tyrol references put Innsbruck apartments around €7,000–7,800/m²; Kitzbühel district >€10,000/m².
rental.adr_occupancy: High in ski resorts; Innsbruck more residential / Good year-round urban/alpine occupancy

price_basis: Built apartment benchmark. Innsbruck 2026 range €7,000–7,800/m²; dashboard uses ~€7,500 × EUR/USD 1.14784 ≈ $8,609/m².
profit_driver: Real city with airport, university, hospitals and alpine access; strong retirement/liveability case.

name: Lake Tahoe
panel_summary: One of the few North American markets that genuinely combines mountain and water, but wildfire, insurance, local regulation, and California/Nevada submarket differences need heavy diligence.
pros: Bay Area and West Coast demand depth
Nevada-side tax appeal for some buyers
cons: fragmented rules across California/Nevada and local jurisdictions
high entry prices for prime lake access
yield can be weak after costs
panel_verdict: Add as the North America mountain-water benchmark, but underwrite risk harder than lifestyle appeal.
ownership_notes: US freehold ownership is open, but rules and tax posture differ materially between California and Nevada sides.
red_flags: Wildfire, insurance availability/cost, STR caps, environmental regulation, and extreme waterfront pricing.

rental.gross_yield: Strong peak-season demand where STRs are permitted, but local caps and risk costs can materially reduce net yield.
rental.revenue: Highly asset-specific by lake access, ski access, and permit zone.
rental.adr_occupancy: Dual ski/summer season, shoulder periods vary.

price_basis: Built-property benchmark for prime but not ultra-waterfront Tahoe product. Approx. $1,150/sq ft blended benchmark, converted to about $12,400/m2; waterfront can be far higher.
price_confidence: Medium-low; large California/Nevada and waterfront/non-waterfront spread
profit_driver: Scarce mountain-water lifestyle, West Coast wealth demand, and Nevada-side tax positioning for some buyers.

name: Jackson Hole
panel_summary: A scarcity and tax-positioning benchmark with exceptional landscape quality, but the panel would be cautious on affordability, liquidity depth below trophy assets, and rental economics.
cons: yield often secondary to wealth preservation
panel_verdict: Add for scarcity comparison, not as a practical yield market. It is a defensive trophy asset case.
ownership_notes: US freehold ownership is open; Wyoming tax posture can be attractive, but foreign buyers need US tax and estate advice.
red_flags: Extreme scarcity pricing, low yield, thin inventory, and reliance on high-net-worth discretionary demand.

rental.gross_yield: Luxury rental demand exists, but low yield relative to capital value and operating constraints should be assumed.
rental.revenue: Asset-specific; verify local manager data and permit status.
rental.adr_occupancy: Summer and winter peaks; shoulder-season variability.

price_basis: Prime Jackson Hole built-property benchmark. Approx. $1,600/sq ft blended benchmark, converted to about $17,200/m2; trophy estates can be far higher.
price_confidence: Medium-low; sales-price opacity and submarket spread
profit_driver: Scarce private land, Wyoming tax appeal, elite outdoor lifestyle, and long-term wealth-preservation demand.

name: Ticino / Lake Lugano
panel_summary: Ticino/Lugano is a strong retirement lifestyle candidate with Swiss safety and Italian flavour. The panel would like it for living quality, but not for yield or easy foreign acquisition.
pros: food and climate are attractive for retirement.
cons: smaller STR/tourism market than major resorts
panel_verdict: Specialist retirement/lifestyle candidate. Good to live in, less compelling as a development investment.
ownership_notes: Foreign-buyer rules still apply unless resident/eligible. Holiday-home permits/quota and Lex Weber/commune rules must be checked.
red_flags: Lex Koller/permit risk, low yield, expensive service costs, smaller STR market than Alpine trophy resorts.

rental.gross_yield: Residential yields are low; retirement/lifestyle and wealth-preservation case dominates
rental.revenue: RealAdvisor June 2026 Lugano average property price around CHF8,367/m²; apartments around CHF8,877/m².
rental.adr_occupancy: Moderate; luxury lake units can price well / Good year-round for long stays

price_basis: Built-property benchmark. Lugano average property price CHF8,367/m² × CHF/USD 1.24288 ≈ $10,400/m².
profit_driver: Swiss safety/healthcare with Italian food/climate feel, Milan access, lake lifestyle.

name: Aspen / Snowmass
panel_summary: A global trophy-ski benchmark rather than a value case. The panel would use Aspen to calibrate scarcity, liquidity, and ultra-prime pricing, but would not treat it as a yield-led destination.
pros: strong luxury liquidity for prime assets
excellent services for a mountain resort
cons: thin inventory and high operating costs
panel_verdict: Keep as a trophy-market benchmark only. Buy for scarcity and personal use, not for income or affordability.
ownership_notes: US freehold ownership is open to foreign buyers, subject to normal tax, financing, reporting, and estate-planning advice.
red_flags: Ultra-prime pricing, low yield, STR limits, wildfire/insurance exposure, and very high carrying costs.

rental.gross_yield: Prime ski homes can command high nightly rates, but entry prices and management/HOA/tax costs compress yield.
rental.revenue: Treat as asset-specific; trophy homes can show strong gross revenue but low yield on capital value.
rental.adr_occupancy: High-season dependent; verify by permit zone and asset type.
rental.confidence: Low-medium; benchmark estimate, not listing-level underwriting

price_basis: Prime Aspen/Snowmass built-property benchmark. Downtown Aspen can trade around $5,000-$6,000/sq ft for nice single-family product; converted to roughly $51,700/m2. Snowmass and condos can vary materially.
price_confidence: Medium for prime benchmark; low-medium across submarkets
profit_driver: Scarcity, global trophy demand, ski/summer seasonality, and resilient ultra-prime buyer depth.

name: Swiss Valais / Vaud Alps
panel_summary: This is the trophy Swiss alpine cluster: extraordinary quality, but structurally unattractive for yield-focused foreign development. The panel would rank it high for prestige and low for investability.
panel_verdict: Do not prioritise for this mandate. Keep only as a luxury benchmark or if personal-use value dominates return objectives.
ownership_notes: Non-resident foreign buyers face Lex Koller quotas/permits and Lex Weber second-home caps. Rules are canton- and commune-specific, and 2026 tightening is under consultation.
red_flags: Foreign-buyer restrictions, very high price, low yield, second-home caps, proposed tightening.

rental.gross_yield: Very low yield after price/tax/management; mainly capital preservation and lifestyle
rental.revenue: UBS Alpine Property Focus 2026 cites Verbier around CHF23,500/m² and Zermatt around CHF21,000/m²; Gstaad/St Moritz even higher.
rental.adr_occupancy: Very high ADR, but availability/licensing and owner usage matter / Strong winter/summer in top resorts

price_basis: Premium alpine benchmark. Verbier CHF23,500/m² × CHF/USD 1.24288 ≈ $29,208/m²; Zermatt CHF21,000/m² ≈ $26,100/m².
profit_driver: Global trophy liquidity, snow reliability at altitude, healthcare/safety, brand value.
